question: [File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker front-run a victim's pending setIsAuthorized transaction by submitting the same Authorization struct with the correct nonce, causing nonce[authorization.authorizer]++ to fire before signature verification, permanently burning the victim's nonce and forcing them to re-sign with nonce+1 under PRECONDITIONS: victim has broadcast a signed Authorization with nonce=N, attacker observes mempool, CALL_SEQUENCE: attacker calls setIsAuthorized(auth, sig) with victim's exact payload before victim's tx lands, violating INVARIANT: a valid signed authorization must be executable exactly once by the intended submitter, causing scoped impact: nonce desync DoS — victim's signed delegation is permanently invalidated without their consent? Proof idea: fuzz nonce=N, front-run with identical calldata, assert victim's subsequent call reverts with InvalidNonce.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker replay a previously consumed Authorization signature on a second EcrecoverAuthorizer instance deployed against the same MIDNIGHT address under PRECONDITIONS: two EcrecoverAuthorizer contracts share the same MIDNIGHT, victim's nonce on instance B is still 0, attacker has victim's old sig from instance A, CALL_SEQUENCE: attacker calls instance_B.setIsAuthorized(auth_with_nonce_0, old_sig), violating INVARIANT: a consumed authorization signature must not grant delegation on any other verifying contract, causing scoped impact: unauthorized delegation — attacker grants themselves full Midnight control over victim's account on instance B? Proof idea: deploy two EcrecoverAuthorizer instances, replay sig from instance A on instance B, assert isAuthorized state changes without victim's consent.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / IdLib.toId] Can an attacker exploit the asymmetry between IdLib.toId using INITIAL_CHAIN_ID and EcrecoverAuthorizer using block.chainid to replay a pre-fork authorization signature on the post-fork chain under PRECONDITIONS: chain forks changing block.chainid, victim signed Authorization on original chain, CALL_SEQUENCE: on forked chain attacker calls setIsAuthorized(auth, original_sig) where block.chainid now differs, violating INVARIANT: EIP-712 domain separator must bind signatures to a single chain, causing scoped impact: cross-chain authorization replay — victim's delegation is activated on the fork chain without their consent, granting attacker full Midnight account control? Proof idea: vm.chainId(newId), replay original sig, assert domain separator mismatch prevents acceptance.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit an Authorization with deadline == block.timestamp (exact boundary) and have it accepted, then immediately use the granted delegation to drain collateral before the next block under PRECONDITIONS: attacker controls block timestamp within validator tolerance, CALL_SEQUENCE: (1) submit setIsAuthorized with deadline=block.timestamp, (2) immediately call withdrawCollateral on behalf of victim in same block, violating INVARIANT: expired authorizations must not grant delegation, causing scoped impact: unauthorized collateral withdrawal — attacker uses boundary-accepted delegation to seize victim's collateral? Proof idea: unit test with block.timestamp == deadline, assert setIsAuthorized succeeds and subsequent withdrawCollateral executes.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit an Authorization with deadline == type(uint256).max to create a permanent never-expiring delegation under PRECONDITIONS: victim signs Authorization with deadline=type(uint256).max, attacker submits it, CALL_SEQUENCE: setIsAuthorized(auth_with_max_deadline, sig), then attacker uses delegation indefinitely, violating INVARIANT: delegated authorization should be time-bounded to limit exposure window, causing scoped impact: permanent unauthorized delegation — attacker retains full Midnight account control over victim indefinitely with no expiry mechanism? Proof idea: fuzz deadline=type(uint256).max, assert no upper bound check exists, assert delegation persists across arbitrary time warps.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker exploit the fact that nonce[authorization.authorizer]++ fires before ecrecover to burn a victim's nonce with a malformed signature (v=27, r=0, s=0 producing address(0)) under PRECONDITIONS: attacker knows victim's current nonce N, CALL_SEQUENCE: attacker calls setIsAuthorized(Authorization{authorizer=victim, nonce=N, ...}, Signature{v=27,r=0,s=0}), violating INVARIANT: nonce must only be consumed by a valid signature from the authorizer or their delegate, causing scoped impact: nonce desync — victim's nonce is incremented without a valid authorization being granted, forcing victim to re-sign? Proof idea: submit zero-signature with correct nonce, assert InvalidSignature reverts but nonce is already incremented.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized delegate of the authorizer sign and submit an Authorization to grant themselves additional authorizations, creating a privilege escalation chain under PRECONDITIONS: victim authorizes operator A in Midnight, operator A signs Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=A, isAuthorized=true} and submits via EcrecoverAuthorizer, CALL_SEQUENCE: (1) victim.setIsAuthorized(A, true), (2) A signs auth granting A→B, (3) A calls ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth, A_sig), violating INVARIANT: authorization grants must only be made by the authorizer themselves or their explicitly trusted delegate, causing scoped impact: unauthorized delegation chain — operator A bootstraps further delegations beyond victim's intent? Proof idea: assert that an authorized signer can sign on behalf of authorizer to grant new authorizations.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized delegate use EcrecoverAuthorizer to revoke the authorizer's own self-authorization or revoke other delegates, effectively locking the authorizer out of their account under PRECONDITIONS: victim authorizes operator A, operator A signs Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=victim, isAuthorized=false}, CALL_SEQUENCE: A calls ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth_revoking_victim, A_sig), violating INVARIANT: an authorized delegate must not be able to revoke the authorizer's own access or other delegates without explicit consent, causing scoped impact: account lockout — victim loses ability to manage their own Midnight state? Proof idea: authorized signer submits revocation of authorizer's self-delegation, assert isAuthorized[victim][victim] becomes false.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit an Authorization with isAuthorized=false for a currently-active delegation to revoke it, then immediately replay the original grant signature (which has a lower nonce) to re-grant it, under PRECONDITIONS: victim has nonce=2, attacker has victim's old grant sig at nonce=0, CALL_SEQUENCE: (1) attacker submits revoke at nonce=2, (2) attacker tries to replay nonce=0 sig, violating INVARIANT: sequential nonces must prevent replay of any prior authorization state, causing scoped impact: authorization state manipulation — confirm sequential nonce correctly blocks replay of old grant after revoke? Proof idea: fuzz nonce ordering, assert old sig at nonce=0 is rejected after nonce advances to 3.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / Midnight.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker who obtains a signed Authorization for isAuthorized=true submit it after the victim has already revoked the delegation via direct Midnight.setIsAuthorized, re-granting the delegation without the victim's current consent under PRECONDITIONS: victim signed auth(nonce=0, isAuthorized=true), victim later called midnight.setIsAuthorized(attacker, false, victim) directly, attacker still holds the signed auth, CALL_SEQUENCE: attacker calls ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth_nonce0, sig), violating INVARIANT: a revoked delegation must not be re-grantable via a stale signature, causing scoped impact: stale-signature delegation reinstatement — attacker regains full account control after victim revoked them? Proof idea: revoke via direct Midnight call, then submit old EcrecoverAuthorizer sig, assert nonce=0 is still valid if never submitted.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker exploit the minimal EIP712Domain (missing name and version fields) to craft a collision with another protocol's domain separator that uses the same keccak256('EIP712Domain(uint256 chainId,address verifyingContract)') typehash under PRECONDITIONS: another deployed contract uses identical minimal domain typehash and same verifyingContract address (e.g., via CREATE2 address collision or same address on different chain), CALL_SEQUENCE: attacker submits a signature intended for the other protocol as an Authorization to EcrecoverAuthorizer, violating INVARIANT: EIP-712 domain separator must uniquely identify the signing context, causing scoped impact: cross-protocol signature confusion — a signature for a different protocol is accepted as a valid Midnight authorization? Proof idea: deploy a contract with identical domain typehash at same address, sign for it, replay on EcrecoverAuthorizer.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit an Authorization where authorization.authorizer == address(0) and ecrecover returns address(0) for a degenerate signature, bypassing the signer != address(0) check because the check fires after the nonce increment under PRECONDITIONS: attacker crafts sig where ecrecover returns address(0), sets authorizer=address(0), CALL_SEQUENCE: setIsAuthorized(Authorization{authorizer=address(0), ...}, degenerate_sig), violating INVARIANT: ecrecover returning address(0) must always revert, causing scoped impact: zero-address authorization — if address(0) is authorizer, any subsequent isAuthorized[address(0)][X] check could be confused? Proof idea: craft degenerate sig, assert InvalidSignature fires before any state change.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker who is currently authorized by the victim use EcrecoverAuthorizer to grant authorization to a fresh address they control, then have the victim revoke the attacker, while the fresh address retains access under PRECONDITIONS: victim authorizes attacker A, A signs Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=B, isAuthorized=true}, A submits it, victim revokes A, CALL_SEQUENCE: (1) A calls ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized granting B, (2) victim revokes A, (3) B still has access, violating INVARIANT: revoking a delegate must transitively remove all delegations that delegate granted, causing scoped impact: persistent unauthorized access — B retains full account control after victim revokes A? Proof idea: multi-step authorization chain, assert B's access persists after A is revoked.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / Midnight.multicall] Can an attacker use multicall to atomically: (1) submit a signed Authorization granting themselves access, (2) call withdrawCollateral on behalf of victim, (3) call setIsAuthorized to revoke themselves, making the attack appear as a no-op in authorization state under PRECONDITIONS: attacker holds a valid signed Authorization from victim, victim has collateral deposited, CALL_SEQUENCE: midnight.multicall([setIsAuthorized_grant, withdrawCollateral, setIsAuthorized_revoke]), violating INVARIANT: authorization state at end of multicall must reflect all intermediate state changes, causing scoped impact: collateral theft via transient authorization — attacker drains collateral within a single multicall leaving no trace in final auth state? Proof idea: multicall combining grant+withdraw+revoke, assert collateral balance decreases despite final auth state appearing unchanged.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified / EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker who is authorized by a maker use EcrecoverAuthorizer to grant themselves authorization, then sign a Merkle root for the maker's offers via EcrecoverRatifier, enabling fills of offers the maker never intended under PRECONDITIONS: maker authorizes attacker in Midnight, attacker signs a Merkle root containing crafted offers, CALL_SEQUENCE: (1) attacker signs root with crafted offers, (2) taker calls midnight.take with ratifierData containing attacker's sig, (3) EcrecoverRatifier checks isAuthorized(maker, attacker)==true, violating INVARIANT: only the maker or their explicitly trusted delegate should be able to authorize offer fills, causing scoped impact: unauthorized offer fill — attacker enables fills of offers maker never signed? Proof idea: authorized signer signs a root with modified offer terms, assert isRatified accepts it.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified] Can an attacker replay a valid EcrecoverRatifier signature (root + Merkle proof) across different markets by constructing offers with different market fields but the same Merkle leaf hash under PRECONDITIONS: maker signed root R for market M1, attacker constructs offer O2 for market M2 that hashes to same leaf as O1, CALL_SEQUENCE: attacker submits take on M2 with ratifierData containing maker's sig for R and proof for O2, violating INVARIANT: a ratifier signature must bind to the exact offer including its market, causing scoped impact: cross-market offer replay — maker's signature authorizes fills in a market they never intended? Proof idea: find or construct two offers with identical hashOffer output across different markets, assert isRatified accepts both.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified] Can an attacker submit a Merkle proof with leafIndex that overflows the proof.length bit-width check (leafIndex >> proof.length == 0) to pass isLeaf validation for a leaf that was never included in the tree under PRECONDITIONS: attacker crafts leafIndex and proof such that the overflow check passes but the path resolves to a different leaf, CALL_SEQUENCE: ecrecoverRatifier.isRatified(offer, abi.encode(sig, root, crafted_leafIndex, crafted_proof)), violating INVARIANT: isLeaf must only return true for leaves genuinely included in the Merkle tree, causing scoped impact: unauthorized offer fill — attacker fills an offer not in the maker's signed tree? Proof idea: fuzz leafIndex and proof.length boundary, assert isLeaf cannot be satisfied for non-member leaves.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.cancelRoot / EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker who is authorized by a maker cancel the maker's Merkle root via cancelRoot, then use EcrecoverAuthorizer to revoke their own authorization, leaving the maker with no way to fill their offers and no record of who canceled under PRECONDITIONS: maker authorizes attacker, attacker calls cancelRoot(maker, root), attacker revokes own authorization, CALL_SEQUENCE: (1) ecrecoverRatifier.cancelRoot(maker, root), (2) midnight.setIsAuthorized(attacker, false, maker), violating INVARIANT: offer cancellation must be traceable to an authorized actor and must not be reversible without maker consent, causing scoped impact: irreversible offer DoS — maker's entire offer tree is permanently canceled by a transient delegate? Proof idea: authorized delegate cancels root, revokes self, assert isRootCanceled[maker][root] is permanently true.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: IdLib.toId / EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker exploit the fact that BaseTest.toId uses block.chainid while Midnight.toId uses INITIAL_CHAIN_ID to construct a market ID that resolves differently in test vs production, causing a test to pass while the production invariant is violated under PRECONDITIONS: test environment has block.chainid == INITIAL_CHAIN_ID (default), test passes, production deployment has different INITIAL_CHAIN_ID after fork, CALL_SEQUENCE: deploy Midnight on forked chain, create market, observe toId diverges from test expectation, violating INVARIANT: market IDs must be consistent between test and production environments, causing scoped impact: accounting corruption — positions and authorizations reference wrong market IDs in production? Proof idea: differential test comparing BaseTest.toId vs midnight.toId after vm.chainId change.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: IdLib.storeInCode / IdLib.toId] Can an attacker on a forked chain create a new market whose SSTORE2 address (derived from INITIAL_CHAIN_ID as CREATE2 salt) collides with an existing market's SSTORE2 address from the original chain, causing toMarket to return wrong market params under PRECONDITIONS: fork changes block.chainid but INITIAL_CHAIN_ID stays the same, attacker creates a new market on the fork with different params but same CREATE2 salt (INITIAL_CHAIN_ID), CALL_SEQUENCE: touchMarket(new_market) on fork, observe storeInCode uses same INITIAL_CHAIN_ID salt, violating INVARIANT: each market's SSTORE2 address must uniquely store its own params, causing scoped impact: market param confusion — toMarket returns wrong collateral/oracle/LLTV for a market ID? Proof idea: fork chain, create two markets with same INITIAL_CHAIN_ID salt, assert storeInCode reverts or collides.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit an Authorization where authorization.authorized == address(ecrecoverAuthorizer) itself, granting the EcrecoverAuthorizer contract full delegation over the victim's Midnight account, enabling any future caller to trigger arbitrary authorizations under PRECONDITIONS: victim signs Authorization{authorized=ecrecoverAuthorizer, isAuthorized=true}, CALL_SEQUENCE: setIsAuthorized(auth, sig), then any caller can call ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized with any new auth signed by ecrecoverAuthorizer (which cannot sign), violating INVARIANT: the authorized address must be a meaningful EOA or contract that can exercise delegation, causing scoped impact: authorization confusion — EcrecoverAuthorizer is granted delegation it cannot exercise, but the grant itself may be exploitable via other paths? Proof idea: grant ecrecoverAuthorizer as authorized, assert no unintended state changes result.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit an Authorization where authorization.authorizer == authorization.authorized (self-authorization) to grant themselves a self-referential delegation, then use it to satisfy the isAuthorized check in EcrecoverAuthorizer for future signatures under PRECONDITIONS: attacker signs Authorization{authorizer=attacker, authorized=attacker, isAuthorized=true, nonce=0}, CALL_SEQUENCE: setIsAuthorized(self_auth, attacker_sig), violating INVARIANT: self-authorization must not create any new privilege beyond what the attacker already has, causing scoped impact: no direct impact but confirm self-authorization does not bypass any downstream check that assumes authorizer != authorized? Proof idea: submit self-auth, assert isAuthorized[attacker][attacker] is set, verify no privilege escalation results.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit multiple Authorization transactions in the same block with consecutive nonces (N, N+1, N+2) all signed by the victim, where the last one revokes a delegation, but a miner reorders them so the revocation lands first and the grant lands last, under PRECONDITIONS: victim signs nonce=0 (grant), nonce=1 (revoke), attacker submits both, miner reorders, CALL_SEQUENCE: submit revoke(nonce=1) before grant(nonce=0) — but sequential nonce prevents this; confirm nonce ordering is strictly enforced, violating INVARIANT: sequential nonces must enforce submission order, causing scoped impact: confirm that nonce ordering prevents miner-reordering attacks on authorization state? Proof idea: attempt out-of-order nonce submission, assert InvalidNonce reverts for nonce=1 before nonce=0.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / Midnight.take] Can an attacker who holds a valid signed Authorization use it to grant themselves delegation, immediately take a borrow offer on behalf of the victim (increasing victim's debt), then revoke the delegation, leaving the victim with unexpected debt under PRECONDITIONS: victim has collateral deposited, attacker holds signed Authorization{authorized=attacker, isAuthorized=true}, CALL_SEQUENCE: (1) setIsAuthorized(grant_auth, sig), (2) midnight.take(borrow_offer, ..., taker=victim, ...), (3) midnight.setIsAuthorized(attacker, false, victim), violating INVARIANT: debt must only be created with the borrower's explicit consent, causing scoped impact: unauthorized debt creation — victim's collateral is encumbered without consent? Proof idea: grant→take→revoke sequence, assert victim's debtOf increases without their direct action.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / Midnight.setConsumed] Can an attacker who obtains delegation via EcrecoverAuthorizer call setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max, victim) to cancel all of the victim's offers in a group, then revoke the delegation, under PRECONDITIONS: attacker holds signed Authorization granting themselves access, victim has active offers in a group, CALL_SEQUENCE: (1) ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(grant, sig), (2) midnight.setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max, victim), (3) revoke, violating INVARIANT: offer cancellation via setConsumed must only be performed by the maker or their explicitly trusted delegate, causing scoped impact: irreversible offer cancellation — victim's entire offer group is permanently canceled by a transient delegate? Proof idea: grant delegation, call setConsumed(max), assert consumed[victim][group] == type(uint256).max.
